topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, instantly sucking the air from my lungs. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party for Sarah is cancelled." The words swam before my eyes, refusing to coalesce into anything solid. A coldness crept up my spine, a paralyzing chill that made my hands slick. I reread the message, checking it three times, half-expecting a trick, a cruel joke played on me. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the sounds of the evening outside. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair backward with a clatter that seemed deafening in the sudden silence of the room. I needed to breathe, but the air felt too thick, too heavy to draw in.

[story 2]

I stared at the notification, my breath catching in a shallow, rapid series of gasps. The festive decorations we’d spent weeks arranging seemed suddenly garish, mocking me. A knot tightened in my stomach, a hard, painful lump that refused to loosen. I leaned back in my seat, my gaze fixed on the screen, willing the information to be false. But the stark, unvarnished text confirmed the worst. My palms grew damp, and I instinctively rubbed them together, the friction a small, desperate attempt to generate some kind of warmth. I started pacing the small space, my steps quick and jerky, my eyes darting toward the door as if expecting someone to materialize and snatch the message away. A tremor ran through my legs, making it hard to keep my balance.

[story 3]

When I finally read the cancellation notice, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. A wave of intense unease washed over me, leaving me rooted to the spot. My throat constricted, making it difficult to produce any sound, and I felt a sudden, overwhelming need to retreat. I slowly lowered my gaze from the glowing screen to the empty room around me. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. Every instinct screamed at me to flee, to run out of this suffocating space, but my feet remained stubbornly planted. I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, a dizzying sensation that made the edges of my vision blur momentarily.
